channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3559628: Allow #[MapQueryParameter] attribute for query string handling in controllers
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 43c2d5e · +172/-222 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3559628 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Drupal now supports mapping query string parameters directly to controller method arguments using the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute from Symfony 6.3. This eliminates repetitive validation code that checked for missing query parameters and manually extracted values from the request object. Controllers become shorter and easier to read, with clearer method signatures that show what parameters they expect.

Impact

  • New API: Controllers can now use the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute to automatically map query string parameters to method arguments.

Technical details

This change adds Symfony's QueryParameterValueResolver to Drupal's argument resolver chain in core.services.yml. The resolver automatically extracts query string parameters and maps them to controller method arguments decorated with the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute.

ThemeController was refactored to demonstrate this pattern. The three methods uninstall(), install(), and setDefaultTheme() previously accepted a Request object, manually extracted the theme query parameter, and validated its presence. Now they accept a string $theme parameter with the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute. The validation code and throws of AccessDeniedHttpException were removed.

Error handling changed slightly. When a required query parameter is missing, the response code changed from 403 to 500, with a more helpful error message from Symfony explaining which argument could not be resolved. This is acceptable because these are error conditions that should not occur during normal operation.

Controllers are explicitly excluded from Drupal's backward compatibility policy, so this method signature change does not require deprecation.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 24, 2025
  • Committed: February 11, 2026 (2 months later)

Collaboration

The discussion between longwave and alexpott about the 403 to 500 response code change shows good judgment about error handling in practice. Rather than treating the status code change as a blocker, they recognized that these error conditions indicate malformed requests that should not happen during normal site operation, and that the new error messages are actually more helpful for debugging. This pragmatic assessment kept the issue moving forward while identifying a related bug in willInstallExperimentalTheme() for follow-up work in #3572785.

Lessons learned

  • When query parameter validation moves from explicit checks to framework-level attribute mapping, errors change from 4xx client errors to 5xx server errors. This is acceptable for error conditions that should never occur in normal operation and improves the specificity of error messages. (via alexpott, longwave)
  • When framework features eliminate boilerplate validation code, the improved developer experience and reduced complexity outweigh concerns about changing error response codes in edge cases that shouldn't occur during normal operation. (via alexpott, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567544: Alias lookup by path is not case sensitive but AliasPrefixList and AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias's return value is leading to inconsistencies
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cd54e64 · +78/-2 prod · +42/-0 test · +24/-0 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3567544 · 4 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Drupal's path alias system had an inconsistency where looking up aliases by system path worked correctly regardless of case (treating /node/1 and /NODE/1 the same), but internal components did not consistently honor this case-insensitive behavior. This led to situations where the alias manager would fail to find an alias when given a path with different capitalization, even though Drupal paths are supposed to be case-insensitive. The fix ensures all parts of the path alias system consistently treat paths as case-insensitive, so aliases resolve correctly regardless of how users or code capitalize the path.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The path alias system uses AliasPrefixList to cache which path prefixes have aliases and AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias() to bulk-load aliases. The bug was that AliasPrefixList performed case-sensitive lookups while AliasRepositoryInterface::lookupBySystemPath() performed case-insensitive ones, creating inconsistent behavior depending on which API was used.

The fix modifies AliasPrefixList to normalize all paths to lowercase using mb_strtolower() in its get(), set(), has(), resolveCacheMiss(), and loadMenuPathRoots() methods. This ensures the prefix cache correctly handles paths regardless of case.

In AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias(), the fix adds logic to track a mapping between user-provided paths and their lowercase equivalents. When the database returns results, if the stored path differs by case from the user-provided path, the method now returns entries for both the stored path and the user-provided path, with the stored path listed first. The interface documentation for AliasRepositoryInterface::preloadPathAlias() was updated to document this dual-entry behavior.

New test coverage was added to AliasTest to verify case-insensitive behavior in testLookupPath() and testPrefixList(), including edge cases where different capitalizations are looked up in sequence.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 14, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (27 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567159: Fix "expects array<string>" issues detected by phpstan
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a7b81e9 · +33/-33 prod · +12/-12 test · 18 files
  • Discussion: #3567159 · 3 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Drupal's codebase now passes stricter PHPStan type analysis for array parameters. The change fixes 25 type declaration errors where functions expected arrays of strings but received arrays with integers or multidimensional arrays. Most fixes update documentation comments to reflect the actual data structures used. In one case, integer values are explicitly cast to strings before being passed to str_replace(). This is part of ongoing work to improve Drupal's static analysis coverage and code quality.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue resolved PHPStan violations detected when enabling the checkFunctionArgumentTypes parameter. PHPStan flagged 25 locations where @param annotations declared array<string> or string[] but the actual data structures were different.

The fixes fall into two categories. Most changes update @param documentation to accurately describe complex array structures. For example, AnnotatedClassDiscovery::$pluginNamespaces changed from string[] to array<string, list<string>> to reflect that it's a keyed array where each value is itself a list of strings. Similarly, test method parameters in ConfigEntityValidationTestBase now declare array<string, array<string, list<string>|string>>|null to describe nested validation error structures.

The other category involves runtime type casting. In Result::render(), the Views result area plugin builds a replacements array for str_replace(). Since str_replace() expects array<string>, integer values like $start, $end, and $total are now cast to strings using (string) before assignment. The final str_replace() call was also simplified to pass $replacements directly instead of calling array_values(), since the array is already properly structured.

These changes only affect internal type documentation and do not alter runtime behavior. The fixes improve PHPStan's ability to catch type errors as Drupal moves toward higher analysis levels.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (28 days later)

Lessons learned

  • When fixing multiple static analysis violations across a codebase, fix as many issues as possible before adding the new rule to the baseline. This keeps violations discoverable by line number rather than requiring baseline regeneration to locate them. (via mstrelan)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572527: \Drupal\DrupalInstalled::VERSIONS_HASH is not consistent for the same set of packages
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f905d6d · +21/-3 prod · +411/-0 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3572527 · 3 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

Drupal generates a version hash to track when installed packages change. This hash was inconsistent because Composer does not always return packages in the same order. Identical sets of packages could produce different hashes, breaking cache invalidation and causing unnecessary rebuilds. This particularly affected multi-architecture Docker builds and other scenarios where packages are installed multiple times. The fix ensures packages are always sorted before calculating the hash, making the result predictable and reliable.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The \Drupal\DrupalInstalled::VERSIONS_HASH constant is generated by DrupalInstalledTemplate::getCode() during Composer operations. This hash is calculated from all installed packages and used to invalidate caches when dependencies change.

The bug occurred because \Composer\Repository\RepositoryInterface::getPackages() does not guarantee a consistent sort order. Running composer remove and composer require on the same package could reorder the list, changing the hash even when the final package set was identical.

The fix adds a usort() call in DrupalInstalledTemplate::getCode() that sorts packages by their unique name using the spaceship operator before passing them to array_reduce(). This ensures the concatenated version string used for hashing always follows the same order.

A new test DrupalInstalledTest was added to verify hash consistency. The test installs packages, adds two fixtures, removes one, then re-adds it, confirming the hash returns to its previous value when the package set is restored.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 10, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3569328: Modernize locale.translations.inc
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b4ee668 · +1088/-376 prod · +18/-20 test · 38 files
  • Discussion: #3569328 · 4 contributors · 37 comments

Summary

The Locale module now uses modern services instead of procedural functions in its translation management system. Functions that handled translation source files, project data, and update options have been reorganized into reusable service classes. This change reduces circular dependencies in the module and makes the code easier to maintain. Site owners who use the Locale module should see no visible changes in functionality.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Functions locale_translation_get_projects(), locale_translation_clear_cache_projects(), locale_translation_load_sources(), locale_translation_build_sources(), locale_translation_source_check_file(), locale_translation_source_build(), locale_translation_build_server_pattern(), locale_cron_fill_queue(), _locale_translation_file_is_remote(), _locale_translation_source_compare(), and _locale_translation_default_update_options() deprecated. Constants LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_LT, LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_EQ, and LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_GT deprecated without replacement. File locale.translation.inc deprecated.
  • New API: New LocaleSource service and LocaleDefaultOptions class; new LocaleProjectStorageInterface::getProjects() method.

Technical details

The issue converted 11 procedural functions from locale.translation.inc into service-based architecture. The main changes organize code into three areas. First, a new LocaleSource service encapsulates functions related to building and checking translation source files: loadSources(), buildSources(), sourceCheckFile(), sourceBuild(), and buildServerPattern(). Second, the existing LocaleProjectStorage service gained a new getProjects() method that replaces locale_translation_get_projects() and handles its own caching through a $projectCache property. Third, a new LocaleDefaultOptions class provides a static updateOptions() method for translation import settings.

The cron queue population logic from locale_cron_fill_queue() moved directly into LocaleHooks::cron(). The function _locale_translation_source_compare() was inlined in TranslationStatusForm::prepareUpdateData() as a simple timestamp comparison. Helper function _locale_translation_file_is_remote() became a protected method in LocaleSource. Three comparison constants (LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_LT, EQ, GT) were deprecated without replacement since they are only used in the inlined comparison logic.

All deprecated functions now trigger deprecation errors and proxy to the new service methods. The deprecated functions targeting removal in 12.0.0 are prefixed with underscore (internal use). Functions in the public API target removal in 13.0.0. The entire locale.translation.inc file triggers a deprecation notice when included. Calls to drupal_static_reset('locale_translation_get_projects') now trigger deprecation and call LocaleProjectStorage::resetCache().

The LocaleProjectStorage::resetCache() method now clears both the existing $cache property and the new $projectCache property. Multiple files that previously required locale.translation.inc now use dependency injection or service locator calls instead.

Upgrade

Module maintainers who call deprecated functions should update to the new services:

Replace locale_translation_get_projects() with:

\Drupal::service('locale.project')->getProjects($project_names);

Replace locale_translation_clear_cache_projects() with:

\Drupal::service('locale.project')->resetCache();

Replace locale_translation_build_sources() with:

\Drupal::service(LocaleSource::class)->buildSources($projects, $langcodes);

Replace locale_translation_source_build() with:

\Drupal::service(LocaleSource::class)->sourceBuild($project, $langcode, $filename);

Replace _locale_translation_default_update_options() with:

LocaleDefaultOptions::updateOptions();

See the change record at https://www.drupal.org/node/3569330 for the complete list of replacements.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 24, 2026
  • Committed: February 9, 2026 (16 days later)

Collaboration

The issue involved significant iteration on deprecation strategy. Contributors nicxvan and dcam collaborated through multiple rounds of manual testing to ensure translation downloads, remote checks, and language enabling all continued working correctly. Comment #22 from dcam noted an opportunity for simplification when reviewing constant usage, leading nicxvan to create a separate merge request. The decision evolved mid-issue on how to handle underscore-prefixed functions, with initial deletion replaced by deprecation following updated BC policy guidance. The final approach deprecated all functions in 11.4 but set different removal versions: 13.0.0 for public API and 12.0.0 for internal functions.

Lessons learned

  • When refactoring procedural code spread across includes, consolidating all changes in a single pass reduces circular dependencies more effectively than incremental migrations. Address the entire include file at once when it sits at the bottom of the dependency chain. (via nicxvan)
  • When reviewing large code migrations, use git diff with --color-moved=dimmed_zebra and --color-moved-ws=ignore-all-space to distinguish relocated code from modified code, making it easier to validate that behavior hasn't changed. (via nicxvan)
  • When deprecating APIs, removal timing depends on visibility: public API functions target the next major after deprecation plus one (11.4 → 13), while internal underscore-prefixed functions target the next major (11.4 → 12). (via nicxvan)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567616: Move _help_search_update to hook
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e35a5a0 · +64/-82 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3567616 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

The Help module's internal code structure has been modernized by moving a helper function into a class method and simplifying duplicated hook implementations. The change removes the procedural function _help_search_update() from help.module and consolidates four similar hook implementations into three. This is part of ongoing work to align Drupal core with modern PHP practices and make code easier to maintain. Site behavior remains identical when installing or uninstalling modules and themes.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The _help_search_update() procedural function has been removed from help.module and converted to a protected searchUpdate() method within the HelpHooks class. This function ensures the help search index is updated when extensions are installed or uninstalled.

Four hook implementations now call searchUpdate() instead of the old function: modulesInstalled(), modulesUninstalled(), and a new combined method. The themesInstalled() and themesUninstalled() hook implementations were identical except for their hook attribute, so they have been merged into a single themesInstallOrUninstall() method that uses both #[Hook('themes_installed')] and #[Hook('themes_uninstalled')] attributes.

The entire help.module file has been deleted since it only contained the helper function. All hook logic now lives in the HelpHooks class, following Drupal's modern hook system architecture. The functionality remains unchanged: when modules or themes are installed or uninstalled, the help search plugin instance updates its topic list and index state.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 15, 2026
  • Committed: February 6, 2026 (22 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567163: Move field_ui form callback to hook class
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d49ef26 · +46/-16 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3567163 · 4 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

Drupal is modernizing its codebase by organizing form callbacks into dedicated hook classes. The Field UI module now uses an object-oriented approach for handling the "Save and manage fields" button submission instead of a standalone procedural function. This is part of a broader effort to make Drupal's code more consistent and maintainable, but does not change how the Field UI works or looks.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Procedural function field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() deprecated in favor of FieldUiHooks::manageFieldFormSubmit() method

Technical details

The field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() procedural function has been moved into the FieldUiHooks class as the manageFieldFormSubmit() method. The logic remains identical: it checks if the triggering element is the save_continue button and redirects to the field overview page if appropriate.

The FieldUiHooks::formAlter() method now references the new method using the self::class . ':manageFieldFormSubmit' syntax instead of the string function name. The old procedural function remains as a deprecated wrapper that triggers an E_USER_DEPRECATED error and delegates to the new method through the service container.

This change aligns with Drupal's ongoing migration to hook classes, consolidating related hook implementations and form callbacks into cohesive class structures. The form submission handler logic itself is unchanged.

Upgrade

If your custom code references field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() directly as a form submit handler, update it to use the class method instead:

// Old:
$form['#submit'][] = 'field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit';

// New:
$form['#submit'][] = \Drupal\field_ui\Hook\FieldUiHooks::class . ':manageFieldFormSubmit';

See #3566774 for full details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2026
  • Committed: February 6, 2026 (24 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566792: Deprecate remaining ckeditor5.module procedural code
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 90f9762 · +510/-270 prod · +46/-6 test · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3566792 · 5 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

Drupal is eliminating procedural code from module files. This change moves the last procedural functions from the CKEditor 5 module into object-oriented methods on a hooks service class. The functions remain callable for now but will trigger deprecation warnings. This change only affects contrib modules that directly call these functions, which is rare. Most sites will see no impact.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Three ckeditor5.module procedural functions deprecated: ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit(), _update_ckeditor5_html_filter(), and _ckeditor5_theme_css() moved to Ckeditor5Hooks service.

Technical details

The change moves three procedural functions from ckeditor5.module to methods on the Ckeditor5Hooks service class:

ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::filterFormatEditFormSubmit(). This form submission handler updates the allowed HTML tags when manually editable tags or styles are configured.

_update_ckeditor5_html_filter() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::updateCkeditor5HtmlFilter(). This AJAX callback handler updates multiple areas of the text format form when switching editors or changing CKEditor 5 settings.

_ckeditor5_theme_css() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::themeCss() and is marked protected with no replacement. This internal function retrieves theme stylesheets for use within CKEditor 5.

The deprecated procedural functions now call the new methods via dependency injection. All internal references (form callbacks, AJAX callbacks, and hook implementations) updated to use the class methods directly. The Ckeditor5Hooks constructor gains four new dependencies: RendererInterface, ConfigFactoryInterface, ThemeExtensionList, and MessengerInterface to support the moved logic.

This change is part of the broader initiative to eliminate .module files from Drupal core.

Upgrade

Only contrib modules that directly call the deprecated functions need to update:

If you call ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit(), inject the Ckeditor5Hooks service and call filterFormatEditFormSubmit() instead.

If you call _update_ckeditor5_html_filter() as a form callback, change the callback reference to Ckeditor5Hooks::updateCkeditor5HtmlFilter.

If you call _ckeditor5_theme_css(), this was an internal function with no direct replacement. The CKEditor Premium module is known to use it and will need an alternative approach.

See the change record at #3570104 for details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 10, 2026
  • Committed: February 5, 2026 (25 days later)

Collaboration

Five contributors collaborated on this issue over 25 comments. The team consolidated three overlapping issues into one to avoid conflicts. During review, the team identified that the CKEditor Premium module calls _ckeditor5_theme_css(), prompting discussion about whether to deprecate or delete the function. The decision to deprecate rather than delete follows the standard practice for this initiative, minimizing disruption even when replacement paths are unclear. This demonstrates thoughtful consideration of downstream impacts during API changes.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571492: Several tests fail on PHP 8.1 on 10.6.x because of requirements issues
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f739f7a · +140/-12 · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3571492 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Several functional tests were failing on PHP 8.1 in Drupal 10.6.x because they incorrectly used a trait method designed to handle PHP version warnings. The tests were calling methods to address requirements problems that did not actually exist in their specific test scenarios. This issue will recur when Drupal 11 sites begin upgrading to PHP 8.5 in preparation for Drupal 12.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The RequirementsPageTrait provides methods to handle situations where Drupal displays requirements warnings during installation or updates, such as when running on older PHP versions. Several test classes were incorrectly calling updateRequirementsProblem() or not properly overriding setUpRequirementsProblem() for their specific test scenarios.

The fix removes incorrect calls to updateRequirementsProblem() from EquivalentUpdateTest where the tests are focused on testing equivalent update logic, not requirements checking. For tests like InstallerBrokenDatabaseCredentialsTest and InstallerBrokenDatabasePortSettingsTest that intentionally test requirements problems, the fix adds empty setUpRequirementsProblem() overrides to prevent the trait's default behavior from interfering.

StandardRecipeInstallTest received two changes. It now disables CSS preprocessing via system.performance configuration to ensure the Claro admin theme can be correctly detected by assertRequirementSummaries(). It also overrides setUpRequirementsProblem() because recipe installation bypasses the normal requirements checking flow.

InstallerExistingConfigNoProfileTest also adds an empty setUpRequirementsProblem() override since this test scenario does not involve requirements checking.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 4, 2026
  • Committed: February 4, 2026

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3463524: editor_post_update_sanitize_image_upload_settings fails on missing text format
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5e89bf9 · +32/-4 prod · +50/-0 test · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3463524 · 7 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

When upgrading from Drupal 10.2.x to 10.3.x or later, sites could fail with a fatal error during database updates if an editor configuration entity referenced a text format that no longer existed. This happened when modules like Webform were uninstalled but left orphaned editor configurations behind. The update process would crash with "Call to a member function getConfigDependencyName() on null," blocking the upgrade. This fix adds defensive checks so the update completes with a warning instead of failing, letting site owners clean up the orphaned configuration later.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The editor_post_update_sanitize_image_upload_settings() post-update hook failed when an Editor entity's format property pointed to a non-existent FilterFormat entity. This happened in Editor::calculateDependencies() when calling $this->getFilterFormat()->getConfigDependencyName() - if getFilterFormat() returned NULL, the chained method call triggered a fatal error.

The fix adds a null check in Editor::calculateDependencies(). When getFilterFormat() returns NULL, the code now skips adding the config dependency and emits a E_USER_WARNING via trigger_error() with a message identifying the orphaned editor configuration. The warning message tells site owners which editor entity is misconfigured and suggests reviewing whether it's still needed.

This scenario occurred when modules like Webform created text formats and editor configurations, then were uninstalled. The text format would be deleted but the editor configuration could remain. The new code handles this gracefully so updates can complete.

A unit test was added to EditorConfigEntityUnitTest to verify that when getFilterFormat() returns NULL, the warning is triggered and no config dependency is added.

Timeline

  • Opened: July 24, 2024
  • Committed: February 4, 2026 (1 year later)

Collaboration

This issue affected multiple sites upgrading to 10.3.x, with the reporter encountering it on three separate instances. The root cause was identified through collaboration between the reporter and maintainers who recognized the pattern from similar issues. The reporter helped diagnose by discovering that orphaned Webform editor configurations were the trigger. The fix was committed to Drupal 10.6.x, 11.x, 11.3.x, and the development branch. A follow-up issue was opened to add additional test coverage for newer branches where PHPUnit's warning expectation API changed.

Lessons learned

  • When config entities reference other entities, the referenced entity may no longer exist (e.g., after module uninstallation). Code calculating dependencies should check for null and handle the missing dependency gracefully with logging rather than fataling, preventing upgrade path failures. (via catch, alexpott)
  • When a fatal error occurs during database updates on a stable release, bump the issue to critical priority since the upgrade path affects all users updating to that version, especially when triggered by common scenarios like installing and uninstalling popular contributed modules. (via catch)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570133: PHPUnit security update
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ac069df · +180/-156 prod · +3/-3 test · 19 files
  • Discussion: #3570133 · 6 contributors · 32 comments

Summary

Drupal core updated its PHPUnit dependency to address a security vulnerability. This is a development-only dependency used for running tests. The vulnerability exists in PHPUnit itself and does not affect production sites. Site owners running custom tests should update their development dependencies to receive the security fix. The update brings PHPUnit to version 11.5.50 on Drupal 11.x, 11.3.x, and 10.6.x branches (which use PHPUnit 9.6.34).

Impact

  • Security: PHPUnit security vulnerability CVE-2026-24765 addressed

Technical details

This issue updates the phpunit/phpunit constraint in composer.json from ^11.5.42 to ^11.5.50 on main, 11.x, and 11.3.x branches, and from ^9.6.13 to ^9.6.34 on the 10.6.x branch. These versions contain fixes for CVE-2026-24765, a security vulnerability in PHPUnit.

Running composer audit on affected branches would have flagged this vulnerability. The update was performed by running composer update -W phpunit/phpunit to allow updating dependencies with the -W flag, then manually updating the constraint in both the root composer.json and core/tests/PHPStan/composer.json files, followed by composer update --lock to regenerate lock files.

The update also pulls in updated versions of related packages including phpunit/php-code-coverage (11.0.12), phpunit/php-file-iterator (5.1.1), sebastian/comparator (6.3.3), and nikic/php-parser (5.7.0) as required by the dependency tree. These are all development-only dependencies with no impact on production code.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 28, 2026
  • First commit: February 2, 2026 (5 days later)
  • Last commit: February 4, 2026 (1 day later)

Collaboration

This issue moved quickly through the process, from opening to first commit in five days. The security team approved handling this as a public issue. Multiple contributors collaborated on creating merge requests for different branches when cherry-picking proved unsuccessful. The team prioritized getting this into the next patch release window, which was seven days away when the issue was bumped to critical priority. The final commits required coordinating the order of merges across branches to avoid unnecessary rebases.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571196: Update symfony/process constraint
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 04602e9 · +74/-74 · 21 files
  • Discussion: #3571196 · 4 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Drupal core now requires a patched version of the Symfony Process component to fix a security vulnerability affecting Windows users. The vulnerability could allow incorrect command argument escaping when using MSYS2 or Git Bash on Windows, potentially leading to unintended file operations. Sites running on Windows with these tools should update immediately. Sites on other platforms are not directly affected but should still update to maintain security best practices.

Impact

  • Security: Fixes CVE-2026-24739, a medium severity vulnerability where incorrect argument escaping under MSYS2/Git Bash on Windows could lead to destructive file operations.

Technical details

This change updates the symfony/process dependency constraint in Drupal core to require patched versions that fix CVE-2026-24739. For Drupal 11.x and 11.3.x running Symfony 7.4, the constraint changes from ^7.4 to ^7.4.5. For Drupal 10.6.x running Symfony 6.4, it changes from ^6.4 to ^6.4.33.

The vulnerability affects the Symfony Process component when running under MSYS2 or Git Bash environments on Windows. These environments provide Unix-like shells on Windows, and improper argument escaping in the Process component could allow command arguments to be interpreted incorrectly, potentially leading to destructive file operations.

The fix was applied to the 10.6.x, 11.3.x, and 11.x branches. The main development branch was intentionally skipped since it is waiting for the full Symfony 8 update tracked in #3570077.

The diff shows updates to core/composer.json and composer/Metapackage/CoreRecommended/composer.json with the new version constraints, along with the corresponding composer.lock updates reflecting the upgraded package versions.

Upgrade

Run composer update to pull in the patched Symfony Process component. No code changes are required in your site or custom modules.

If you maintain a custom composer.json file that pins specific Symfony component versions, ensure your constraints allow the updated versions: symfony/process:^7.4.5 for Drupal 11.x or ^6.4.33 for Drupal 10.x.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 3, 2026
  • Committed: February 4, 2026 (1 day later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3560398: Move _dblog_get_message_types and dblog_filters to a DbLogFilters service and deprecate them
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 51c8bbe · +254/-122 · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3560398 · 8 contributors · 36 comments

Summary

The Database Logging module now uses a service for filter operations instead of procedural functions. This modernizes the codebase by moving legacy functions into a proper service class that can be dependency-injected. The old dblog.admin.inc include file is no longer needed and will be removed in the next major version. This change makes the code easier to test, maintain, and extend while keeping backward compatibility during the transition period.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Three dblog procedural functions moved to DbLogFilters service: _dblog_get_message_types() and dblog_filters() deprecated in Drupal 11.4, removed in Drupal 13; dblog.admin.inc file deprecated in 11.4, removed in Drupal 12

Technical details

Two procedural functions that handled database log filtering have been moved into a new DbLogFilters service class. The _dblog_get_message_types() function becomes the getMessageTypes() method, and the dblog_filters() function becomes the filters() method. Both methods retain identical functionality.

The new service is registered in dblog.services.yml with autowiring enabled, allowing automatic dependency injection of the database connection. It uses StringTranslationTrait to handle translatable strings that were previously handled by the t() function.

Three classes now inject and use the new service: DbLogController injects it through constructor parameters, while DblogFilterForm and the DblogTypes Views plugin both add constructors to receive the service. The controller no longer needs to load dblog.admin.inc via ModuleHandlerInterface::loadInclude().

The deprecated procedural functions now trigger E_USER_DEPRECATED errors and immediately delegate to the new service methods via \Drupal::service(). The dblog.admin.inc file itself triggers a deprecation error when loaded. All deprecated code will be removed in Drupal 13.0 (functions) or 12.0 (include file).

Upgrade

If your custom code calls _dblog_get_message_types() or dblog_filters(), inject the DbLogFilters service and call the corresponding methods instead:

// Before:
$types = _dblog_get_message_types();
$filters = dblog_filters();

// After:
$types = \Drupal::service(\Drupal\dblog\DbLogFilters::class)->getMessageTypes();
$filters = \Drupal::service(\Drupal\dblog\DbLogFilters::class)->filters();

In classes with dependency injection, add the service to your constructor parameters. If you manually include dblog.admin.inc, remove those includes and inject the service instead.

See #3560399 for full details.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 28, 2025
  • Committed: February 3, 2026 (2 months later)

Collaboration

This issue took over two months from opening to commit, with eight contributors collaborating across 36 comments. The discussion explored whether to use a service or a trait for the refactored code, with contributors thoughtfully debating the tradeoffs. The team ultimately chose the service approach as simpler and more practical.

Early feedback questioned whether exposing the previously private _dblog_get_message_types() function as public API was appropriate. The group concluded the specific, stable functionality posed minimal risk. Reviewers provided detailed code suggestions that improved constructor formatting and fixed a copy-paste error in deprecation messages. Multiple rounds of environmental test failures required patience before getting clean pipeline results.

Lessons learned

  • When converting underscore-prefixed 'private' functions to services, making them public API is acceptable. Nothing truly prevented their use before, and if they're called by multiple parts of the system, the service pattern provides cleaner dependency injection without adding complexity like traits. (via nicxvan, smustgrave, mstrelan)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3529464: Make menu trail behaviour in SystemMenuBlock optional
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ba11023 · +318/-14 prod · +494/-58 test · 26 files
  • Discussion: #3529464 · 10 contributors · 74 comments

Summary

Menu blocks can now skip computing the active trail when menus show the same links on every page. Many themes do not style active menu trails in header or footer navigation, yet Drupal still calculates which menu items lead to the current page. This calculation requires database queries and creates separate cache entries for each unique active trail. The new option significantly reduces database queries and cache storage when enabled on menus that display consistently across pages, particularly benefiting sites with large navigation structures.

Impact

  • Config change: New optional ignore_active_trail boolean in menu block settings allows skipping active trail calculation when menu shows all items expanded or only first level.

Technical details

The SystemMenuBlock plugin now supports an optional ignore_active_trail configuration property that skips active trail computation and omits the route.menu_active_trails cache context. The feature only applies when the menu block is configured to show all items expanded or when displaying only the first level with a depth of 1, since these configurations produce identical link structures regardless of the current page.

The block configuration form adds a checkbox labeled "Add a CSS class to ancestors of the current page" that controls this behavior. Form states make the checkbox required when active trail calculation is necessary for correct menu rendering. The form element name is add_active_trail_class (positive statement) while the configuration key is ignore_active_trail (negative statement) to simplify both UI and API logic. The blockSubmit() method inverts the form value before saving.

The shouldSetActiveTrail() method centralizes the logic determining when active trail processing is required. The getCacheContexts() method conditionally includes the active trail context based on this logic. A custom IgnoreActiveTrailConstraint validates that the configuration property is only set to TRUE when compatible with other menu settings.

The configuration schema marks ignore_active_trail with requiredKey: false, allowing gradual adoption without requiring an upgrade path for existing blocks. When unset, the property defaults to FALSE (active trail enabled). The Umami profile's header, footer, and account menu blocks enable this optimization, demonstrating performance improvements in test results with reductions of 12+ queries per request.

Upgrade

No action required. Existing menu blocks continue calculating active trails as before. To optimize performance, edit menu blocks that show all items expanded or only the first level and uncheck "Add a CSS class to ancestors of the current page" if your theme does not style the active trail differently on each page.

The change record at #3533514 provides additional details.

Timeline

  • Opened: June 10, 2025
  • Committed: February 3, 2026 (7 months later)

Collaboration

This issue took seven months and involved 10 contributors across 74 comments. The usability team reviewed the interface twice (#3536866, #3540379), pushing for clearer language that avoids introducing the arcane "active trail" terminology to site builders. The team arrived at "Add a CSS class to ancestors of the current page" as a compromise that explains the feature without technical jargon. The decision to use an optional configuration property rather than a required upgrade path reflects ongoing exploration of "just-in-time" configuration update strategies referenced in #3530727 and #3521221. Performance test improvements validated the optimization, showing reductions from 218 to 205 queries on cold cache and 73 to 61 on cool cache for Umami's front page.

Lessons learned

  • When caching happens at multiple layers, cache contexts that don't affect the output still fragment the cache. For blocks that display the same markup regardless of context (like fully-expanded menus ignoring active trail), omitting unnecessary cache contexts consolidates identical variations into a single cache entry. (via catch)
  • When adding new configuration properties where NULL and a default value should be treated identically, making the property optional (not required in schema) avoids the complexity of update paths across multiple storage implementations (config entities, Layout Builder overrides, exported config). The system can gradually introduce the property on save while maintaining backward compatibility. (via catch, godotislate, alexpott)
  • When designing configuration UI, avoid exposing internal technical concepts like 'active trail' directly to users. Frame options in terms of visible outcomes ('show the same markup on every page' or 'add a CSS class to parent links') rather than system implementation details. (via catch, benjifisher)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568101: Remove editor.module underscore functions in 12.x, deprecate in 11.x. Move the logic in EditorHooks
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 78087df · +341/-164 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3568101 · 4 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Drupal is cleaning up legacy code to prepare for removing .module files. Five internal helper functions in the Editor module that tracked file usage in text fields have been moved into a protected class method. These functions were never intended for external use and were marked with underscore prefixes to signal they were internal. The functions are deprecated in Drupal 11.4 and removed entirely in Drupal 12.0. Site editors and content creators will see no changes. The Editor module continues to track files embedded in formatted text exactly as before.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Five underscore-prefixed functions in editor.module are deprecated in Drupal 11.4 and removed in Drupal 12.0, with no replacement provided.

Technical details

The Editor module provided five underscore-prefixed functions in editor.module to track file usage when images and other files are embedded in formatted text fields. According to Drupal's backward compatibility policy, underscore-prefixed functions are considered internal and not public API.

These functions have been moved to protected methods in the EditorHooks class: _editor_record_file_usage() becomes EditorHooks::recordFileUsage(), _editor_delete_file_usage() becomes EditorHooks::deleteFileUsage(), _editor_get_file_uuids_by_field() becomes EditorHooks::getFileUuidsByField(), _editor_get_formatted_text_fields() becomes EditorHooks::getFormattedTextFields(), and _editor_parse_file_uuids() becomes EditorHooks::parseFileUuids().

In Drupal 11.4, the original functions remain but trigger deprecation notices referencing change record #3568136. In Drupal 12.0, the functions are completely removed from editor.module. The protected methods in EditorHooks now handle all file usage tracking internally when entities are inserted, updated, or deleted.

The hook implementation methods (entityInsert(), entityUpdate(), entityDelete(), and entityRevisionDelete()) now call the new protected methods directly. Documentation examples in entity.api.php that used these functions have been replaced with generic examples.

Upgrade

Contributed modules calling any of these functions should stop using them immediately. These were never public API and should not have been used outside the Editor module.

If your code calls _editor_record_file_usage(), _editor_delete_file_usage(), _editor_get_file_uuids_by_field(), _editor_get_formatted_text_fields(), or _editor_parse_file_uuids(), remove those calls. There is no direct replacement because these methods are now protected. If you need similar functionality, implement your own file tracking logic or file an issue to request a public API.

See #3568136 for the change record.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 17, 2026
  • Committed: February 3, 2026 (16 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566888: Move contact module form callbacks from procedural to hook class
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 47ae02b · +112/-24 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3566888 · 3 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

Drupal continues modernizing its codebase by moving form submission logic out of procedural functions and into object-oriented hook classes. The Contact module now handles form submissions for user profile and account settings through methods in its hooks class. This makes the code more maintainable and follows current best practices. Site functionality remains unchanged, but custom code calling the old functions should update to use the new class methods.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Two procedural functions contact_user_profile_form_submit() and contact_form_user_admin_settings_submit() deprecated in favor of ContactFormHooks class methods.

Technical details

This change moves two form submission handlers from contact.module to the ContactFormHooks class. The contact_user_profile_form_submit() function becomes ContactFormHooks::profileFormSubmit(), and contact_form_user_admin_settings_submit() becomes ContactFormHooks::userAdminSettingsSubmit().

The old procedural functions are now deprecated but remain in place as wrappers. They trigger a deprecation notice and delegate to the hook class methods via service instantiation using \Drupal::service(ContactFormHooks::class). The actual form alter hooks now register the class methods as submit handlers using self::class . ':profileFormSubmit' instead of the function name strings.

The implementation also includes a detailed comment explaining why #config_target cannot be used for the user admin settings form. During validation, #config_target triggers validation of the entire contact.settings config object, but the default default_form value of 'feedback' references a contact form entity that does not ship with the module, causing validation failures.

A minor bug was also fixed: the ContactFormHooks constructor had configFactoryInterface (lowercase 'c') corrected to ConfigFactoryInterface.

Upgrade

If your custom code directly calls contact_user_profile_form_submit() or contact_form_user_admin_settings_submit(), update to use the ContactFormHooks service methods instead.

// Old:
contact_user_profile_form_submit($form, $form_state);

// New:
\Drupal::service(ContactFormHooks::class)->profileFormSubmit($form, $form_state);

See the change record at #3566774 for full details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 11, 2026
  • Committed: February 3, 2026 (22 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3505370: Remove 'filter tips' page and deprecate the long format API
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 03b4ba4 · +66/-68 prod · +58/-28 test · +2/-0 infra · 28 files
  • Discussion: #3505370 · 9 contributors · 24 comments

Summary

Drupal now deprecates the filter tips page that shows examples of HTML formatting. This page appeared at /filter/tips and linked from every text field by default. With modern rich text editors like CKEditor 5, these technical HTML examples are no longer relevant or helpful. Site administrators can add formatting guidance directly in field descriptions, which provides more flexibility and can include links to external resources. The filter tips page looked dated and had no way to hide it through the user interface.

Impact

  • API change: The $long parameter removed from FilterInterface::tips() and FilterBase::tips() method signatures
  • Deprecation: The /filter/tips page, filter.tips and filter.tips_all routes, FilterController::filterTips(), the $long parameter to FilterInterface::tips() and _filter_tips(), and the 'About text formats' link on text fields are deprecated and will be removed in Drupal 12

Technical details

The change deprecates the entire "long format" filter tips system while keeping the short format tips that appear inline on forms when multiple text formats are available. The filter.tips and filter.tips_all routes are deprecated in filter.routing.yml and will be removed in Drupal 12. The FilterController::filterTips() method is also deprecated.

The $long parameter has been removed from the FilterInterface::tips() and FilterBase::tips() method signatures. The _filter_tips() function no longer accepts a $long parameter. When legacy code calls these methods with the $long parameter, Drupal uses reflection to detect the parameter count and triggers deprecation errors while maintaining backward compatibility.

The "About text formats" help link has been removed from TextFormat::processFormat() in the filter element, so it no longer appears below text fields. The menu link definition for filter.tips_all has been removed from filter.links.menu.yml.

Tests that access the /filter/tips routes now use the #[IgnoreDeprecations] attribute. The UserBlocksTest was updated to use node paths instead of the deprecated /filter/tips path for testing login behavior.

Upgrade

Custom filter plugins that implement FilterInterface::tips() should remove the $long parameter from their method signature:

// Before:
public function tips($long = FALSE) {
  if ($long) {
    return $this->t('Detailed explanation...');
  }
  return $this->t('Short tip');
}

// After:
public function tips() {
  return $this->t('Short tip');
}

If you have custom code that calls _filter_tips() or FilterInterface::tips() with the $long parameter, remove that parameter from your calls. Only short tips are now used.

See the change record at #3567879 for complete details.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 9, 2025
  • Committed: February 2, 2026 (11 months later)

Collaboration

This issue involved 9 contributors over 24 comments and nearly a year from opening to commit. The change started with a clear problem statement showing how the filter tips page was redundant in the modern CKEditor 5 era and had an outdated appearance. During development, the scope was refined to focus on removing the "long format" tips and the standalone page while keeping the inline "short format" tips that help distinguish between multiple text formats. The committer noted it felt odd to add deprecation code to the main branch that would soon be removed in Drupal 12, acknowledging this as part of the deprecation process. As a bonus, removing this open path will help prevent fingerprinting tools from identifying Drupal installations.

Lessons learned

  • When a feature becomes obsolete due to architectural shifts (like WYSIWYG editors replacing manual markup), remove it even if functional. Keeping redundant features creates maintenance burden and confuses users about which approach to use. (via catch, lauriii)
  • When deprecating methods from interfaces, remove the method entirely from the interface in a minor release but keep it in base classes for removal in the next major. This allows implementations to stop providing the method while preventing fatal errors in contrib that still calls it. (via catch)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3537962: EntityFieldManager::getFieldDefinitions() per-bundle caching can be expensive
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 79ed737 · +66/-14 prod · +56/-40 test · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3537962 · 5 contributors · 23 comments

Summary

Sites with many entity bundles, such as those using the Webform or Paragraphs modules, now experience faster page loads when caches are empty or cold. The system now rebuilds field definitions for all bundles at once instead of making hundreds of separate cache operations. This reduces the impact on site performance during cache rebuilds, especially noticeable on sites with dozens or hundreds of webforms or paragraph types. The optimization targets cold cache scenarios where every cache miss previously triggered a separate write operation.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The EntityFieldManager::getFieldDefinitions() method previously cached field definitions one bundle at a time. When a cache miss occurred for one bundle, it would rebuild and cache only that bundle's definitions. On sites with many bundles (100+ webforms, 50+ paragraph types), this resulted in hundreds of individual cache set operations during cold cache requests.

The method now anticipates that when field definitions are requested for one bundle, they will likely be needed for all bundles of that entity type during the same request. When a cache miss occurs, getFieldDefinitions() now fetches all bundles from EntityTypeBundleInfoInterface::getBundleInfo(), builds field definitions for all of them by calling buildBundleFieldDefinitions() in a loop, and writes all cache entries at once using CacheBackendInterface::setMultiple().

The implementation maintains backward compatibility by still supporting field definition requests for non-existent bundles, though this is marked with a TODO for future deprecation. Each bundle still gets its own cache entry with the same cache ID format, but they are now read and written as a batch when any single bundle experiences a cache miss.

Performance test updates show the optimization saves approximately 10 cache gets and sets on Umami, with proportionally larger savings on sites with more bundles (99 operations saved for 100 webforms).

Timeline

  • Opened: July 24, 2025
  • Committed: February 2, 2026 (6 months later)

Lessons learned

  • When a system performs identical operations in a loop creating many small cache entries, you can reduce I/O by batching the work and writing all cache entries at once. The cost of loading slightly more data on cache hits is usually negligible compared to the overhead of hundreds of separate cache operations during cold cache scenarios. (via catch)
  • When optimizing caching for batch operations, consider that cache misses may not always affect all entries simultaneously. Designing for cold-cache scenarios (where all items are missing) may do unnecessary work when only single entries are evicted, though this tradeoff is usually acceptable when items are accessed frequently. (via berdir)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566424: Deprecate Views::pluginManager() and Views::handlerManager() and replace with service locator
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 26a0c06 · +182/-124 prod · +158/-4 test · 62 files
  • Discussion: #3566424 · 5 contributors · 18 comments

Summary

Two static helper methods for accessing Views plugin managers are now deprecated. The Views::pluginManager() and Views::handlerManager() methods were simple wrappers that made it easy to get plugin managers by type, but they prevented proper dependency injection. Core now uses a service locator pattern internally, which provides better performance and cleaner architecture. This change is part of ongoing work to remove static service calls and support dependency injection throughout the Views system.

Impact

  • Deprecation: Static methods Views::pluginManager() and Views::handlerManager() deprecated; use \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.views.{type}') or \Drupal::service('views.plugin_managers')->get($type) instead

Technical details

The Views::pluginManager() and Views::handlerManager() static methods in \Drupal\views\Views are now deprecated. These methods previously called \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.views.' . $type) to dynamically fetch a plugin manager service by type.

Core has replaced all internal uses of these deprecated methods with direct service calls. A new views.plugin_managers service locator was added in views.services.yml that provides access to all 19 Views plugin manager services (access, area, argument, argument_default, argument_validator, cache, display, display_extender, exposed_form, field, filter, join, pager, query, relationship, row, sort, style, and wizard).

The implementation now triggers E_USER_DEPRECATED errors and delegates to the service locator using \Drupal::service('views.plugin_managers')->get($type). This service locator pattern provides better performance by lazy-loading plugin managers and enables future work to inject dependencies into display plugins and other Views classes.

All 43 call sites in core were updated to use either the specific plugin manager service (e.g., \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.views.join')) when the type is known at development time, or the service locator (e.g., \Drupal::service('views.plugin_managers')->get($handler_type)) when the type is dynamic. Unit tests were updated to mock the service locator, and two new legacy tests verify the deprecation messages.

Upgrade

If your custom Views code uses Views::pluginManager() or Views::handlerManager(), replace these calls with direct service access. For known plugin types, use the specific service:

// Before:
$join = Views::pluginManager('join')->createInstance('standard', $configuration);

// After:
$join = \Drupal::service('plugin.manager.views.join')->createInstance('standard', $configuration);

For dynamic types where you determine the plugin type at runtime, use the service locator:

// Before:
$handler = Views::handlerManager($handler_type)->getHandler($item);

// After:
$handler = \Drupal::service('views.plugin_managers')->get($handler_type)->getHandler($item);

If your code uses proper dependency injection, inject the specific plugin manager services you need or inject the views.plugin_managers service locator. See the change record for full details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 8, 2026
  • Committed: February 2, 2026 (24 days later)

Collaboration

This issue was split from a larger effort to inject dependencies into Views display plugins (#2015121), where the service locator pattern was first proposed. The initial merge request from that parent issue provided the reference implementation.

Five contributors collaborated over three weeks to complete this deprecation. The work involved careful analysis of all 43 call sites in core to determine whether each could use a specific service or needed dynamic lookup. Parts of the implementation were assisted by AI tooling (Claude Code), demonstrating how automation can help with large-scale refactoring tasks while human reviewers ensure correctness.

The issue was tagged for a change record, which was created and linked to the merge request before commit. A follow-up tag was added to track future work on converting more code to use proper dependency injection rather than static service calls.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3555532: Since symfony/http-foundation 7.4: Request::get() is deprecated, use properties ->attributes, query or request directly instead.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 2b08e5b · +72/-80 prod · +28/-26 test · +0/-338 infra · 48 files
  • Discussion: #3555532 · 7 contributors · 32 comments

Summary

Drupal core now prepares for Symfony 8 compatibility by replacing the deprecated Request::get() method. Symfony deprecated this method because it blurred the source of input data, creating potential security risks. Drupal now explicitly checks the correct parameter bags (query, request, or attributes) for each use case. The change also consolidates handling of AJAX page state by storing it in request attributes early in the request lifecycle, making the code clearer and more maintainable. All tests pass and the behavior remains the same for site owners and developers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Symfony Request::get() method is deprecated in Symfony 7.4 and will be removed in Symfony 8.0. This method checked multiple parameter bags in sequence (attributes, query, request), which made the origin of data unclear and potentially unsafe.

Drupal core replaced all uses of Request::get() with explicit calls to the appropriate parameter bag. For query string parameters, code now calls $request->query->get(). For POST data, code now calls $request->request->get(). For values that could come from either source, code checks both explicitly. The AjaxPageState middleware was enhanced to copy the final AJAX page state into $request->attributes, making it available as a request attribute throughout the request lifecycle.

The most significant changes are in ViewAjaxController::ajaxView(), which now checks both query and request bags for view parameters, and in AjaxBasePageNegotiator, AjaxResponseAttachmentsProcessor, HtmlResponseAttachmentsProcessor, and CSS/JS asset optimizers, which now read ajax_page_state from request attributes instead of using the ambiguous get() method. The FormBuilder now explicitly checks both bags when validating form IDs for AJAX requests.

Tests in AjaxPageStateTest and AjaxBasePageNegotiatorTest were updated to set values in the attributes bag to match the new behavior. The workaround code in ViewAjaxController that temporarily copied theme information to request attributes is now removed since the middleware handles this consistently.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 1, 2025
  • First commit: January 29, 2026 (2 months later)
  • Last commit: February 1, 2026 (3 days later)

Collaboration

This issue involved 7 contributors over 3 months with 32 comments and 2 commits. The initial investigation by longwave identified the deprecation and linked to the Symfony PR explaining the security rationale. Contributors scott_euser and godotislate collaborated to resolve edge cases, particularly around ajax_page_state handling in Views. The team discovered that previous workarounds for AJAX theme negotiation could be consolidated into the AjaxPageState middleware, leading to cleaner code. A change record was created to document the new ajax_page_state request attribute pattern. The discussion also surfaced potential future improvements like moving AJAX metadata to custom headers instead of query parameters.

Lessons learned

  • When accepting user input via HTTP requests, explicitly check the correct parameter bag (query, request, or attributes) rather than using generic getters that check multiple sources. Blurring the origin of input data creates security risks where attackers can smuggle data through unexpected HTTP methods. (via longwave, scott_euser)
  • When upstream dependencies deprecate dangerous patterns for security reasons, investigate their reasoning before deciding on a migration strategy. The upstream rationale often reveals architectural improvements beyond simple API replacement. (via longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
